subject: Chemistry
question: Avogadro number is :
options: Number of atoms in one gram of the element
Number of millilitre which one mole of a gaseous substance occupies at NTP (1 atm & 0℃)
Number of molecules present in one gram molecular mass of a substance.
solution:
Avogadro number means the number of particles present in $1$ mole of a substance.

Now check the options:

Option A: Number of atoms in one gram of the element

This is not always correct. It should be number of atoms in one gram atomic mass, not just one gram.

Option B: Number of millilitre which one mole of a gaseous substance occupies at NTP

This is about molar volume of a gas, not Avogadro number. Also at NTP, $1$ mole gas occupies about $22.4$ litres, which is a volume, not a number of particles.

Option C: Number of molecules present in one gram molecular mass of a substance

This is correct because one gram molecular mass means $1$ mole, and one mole contains Avogadro number of molecules.

Option D: All are correct

This is wrong because A and B are not correct.

So, the correct answer is:

$$\text{Option C}$$

Avogadro number $= 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles.

subject: Chemistry
q: An alkane in the gaseous state is burnt in oxygen. The volumes of O2 required for complete combustion and CO2 produced are in the ratio 7 : 4. Find the molecular formula of the alkane.
question: An alkane in the gaseous state is burnt in oxygen. The volumes of O2 required for complete combustion and CO2 produced are in the ratio 7 : 4. Find the molecular formula of the alkane.
solution:
For an alkane, the general formula is

$$ C_nH_{2n+2} $$

On complete combustion,

$$ C_nH_{2n+2} + \frac{3n+1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow nCO_2 + (n+1)H_2O $$

Now compare the volumes of $O_2$ used and $CO_2$ formed.

Since gases combine in the ratio of their coefficients, we get

$$ O_2 : CO_2 = \frac{3n+1}{2} : n $$

Given ratio is

$$ O_2 : CO_2 = 7 : 4 $$

So,

$$ \frac{3n+1}{2n} = \frac{7}{4} $$

$$ 4(3n+1)=14n $$

$$ 12n+4=14n $$

$$ 4=2n $$

$$ n=2 $$

So the alkane is

$$ C_2H_6 $$

Correct option: A, $C_2H_6$

subject: Chemistry
q: How many moles of electron weigh one kilogram :
question: How many moles of electron weigh one kilogram :
solution:
Mass of one electron is

$$ m_e = 9.108 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg} $$

So, number of electrons in $1\ \text{kg}$ will be

$$ \frac{1}{9.108 \times 10^{-31}} = \frac{1}{9.108} \times 10^{31} $$

Now, $1$ mole contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ electrons.

So, number of moles of electrons in $1\ \text{kg}$ is

$$ \frac{\frac{1}{9.108} \times 10^{31}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{9.108 \times 6.023} \times 10^{8} $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\frac{1}{9.108 \times 6.023} \times 10^{8}} $$

Option D

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of atoms in 560 g of Fe (atomic mass 56 gmol-1) is:
question: Number of atoms in 560 g of Fe (atomic mass 56 gmol-1) is:
solution:
Number of moles of iron in $560\ \text{g}$ is

$$ \frac{560}{56} = 10\ \text{mol} $$

So, number of atoms in $560\ \text{g}$ Fe $= 10N_A$.

Now check the options.

For $70\ \text{g}$ of N:

Atomic mass of N $= 14\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$ \text{Moles of N} = \frac{70}{14} = 5\ \text{mol} $$

So, number of atoms $= 5N_A$.

Twice of this is

$$ 2 \times 5N_A = 10N_A $$

This matches the number of atoms in iron.

So, Option A is correct.

Now for $20\ \text{g}$ of H:

Atomic mass of H $= 1\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$

$$ \text{Moles of H} = \frac{20}{1} = 20\ \text{mol} $$

So, number of atoms $= 20N_A$.

Half of this is

$$ \frac{20N_A}{2} = 10N_A $$

This also matches the number of atoms in iron.

So, Option B is also correct.

Hence, the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C: Both (A) and (B)}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A certain compound has the molecular formula $X_4O_6$. If 10 g of $X_4O_6$ has 5.72 g X, then atomic mass of X is:
question: A certain compound has the molecular formula $X_4O_6$. If 10 g of $X_4O_6$ has 5.72 g X, then atomic mass of X is:
solution:
In $10 \, \text{g}$ of $X_4O_6$, mass of $X = 5.72 \, \text{g}$.

So, mass of oxygen in $10 \, \text{g}$ compound is

$$ 10 - 5.72 = 4.28 \, \text{g} $$

Now in the formula $X_4O_6$,

  • mass of $4$ atoms of $X = 5.72$

  • mass of $6$ atoms of $O = 4.28$

We know atomic mass of oxygen is $16$.

So mass of $6$ oxygen atoms $= 6 \times 16 = 96$

This means,

$$ 4X : 96 = 5.72 : 4.28 $$

$$ \frac{4X}{96} = \frac{5.72}{4.28} $$

$$ 4X = 96 \times \frac{5.72}{4.28} $$

$$ 4X \approx 128.37 $$

$$ X \approx \frac{128.37}{4} \approx 32.09 $$

So atomic mass of $X$ is approximately

$$ 32 \, \text{amu} $$

Option A: $32 \, \text{amu}$

subject: Chemistry
q: An empty container maintained at a constant temperature of 25°C shows a pressure of 1 atm after 2 g of gas A is injected. When an additional 3 g of gas B is included in the same container, the total pressure rises to 1.5 atm. Considering both gases act ideally, calculate the ratio of their molar masses ($M_A : M_B$).
question: An empty container maintained at a constant temperature of 25°C shows a pressure of 1 atm after 2 g of gas A is injected. When an additional 3 g of gas B is included in the same container, the total pressure rises to 1.5 atm. Considering both gases act ideally, calculate the ratio of their molar masses ($M_A : M_B$).
solution:
For a fixed flask at the same temperature, pressure is directly proportional to the number of moles.

So for gas $A$,

$$ P_A = 1 \text{ atm} $$

and number of moles of $A$ is

$$ n_A = \frac{2}{M_A} $$

Since $P \propto n$, when gas $B$ is added, total pressure becomes $1.5$ atm.

So pressure due to gas $B$ alone is

$$ P_B = 1.5 - 1 = 0.5 \text{ atm} $$

Now,

$$ n_B = \frac{3}{M_B} $$

Again, at same $T$ and $V$,

$$ \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{n_A}{n_B} $$

So,

$$ \frac{1}{0.5} = \frac{2/M_A}{3/M_B} $$

$$ 2 = \frac{2M_B}{3M_A} $$

$$ 6M_A = 2M_B $$

$$ M_B = 3M_A $$

Hence,

$$ M_A : M_B = 1 : 3 $$

Option A is correct.

subject: Chemistry
question: The weight of $1 \times 10^{22}$ molecules of $\mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O}$ is
solution:
To find the weight of $1 \times 10^{22}$ molecules of $\mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O}$, we first need its molar mass.

$$ \text{Molar mass of } \mathrm{CuSO_4 \cdot 5H_2O} = 63.5 + 32 + 4(16) + 5(18) $$

$$ = 63.5 + 32 + 64 + 90 = 249.5 \text{ g/mol} $$

Now, $1$ mole contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules.

So, mass of $1 \times 10^{22}$ molecules is

$$ \text{Mass} = \frac{1 \times 10^{22}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \times 249.5 $$

$$ = \frac{249.5}{60.22} \approx 4.14 \text{ g} $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{4.14 \text{ g}} $$

Option A

subject: Chemistry
q: A gaseous mixture contains O2 and N2 in the ratio of 1:4 by weight. The ratio of their number of molecules is
question: A gaseous mixture contains O2 and N2 in the ratio of 1:4 by weight. The ratio of their number of molecules is
solution:
Take the masses in the given ratio:

$$ \text{Mass of } O_2 : \text{Mass of } N_2 = 1:4 $$

Number of molecules is proportional to number of moles.

Now,

$$ \text{Moles} = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} $$

Molar mass of $O_2 = 32$ g/mol

Molar mass of $N_2 = 28$ g/mol

So,

$$ \text{Moles of } O_2 : \text{Moles of } N_2 = \frac{1}{32} : \frac{4}{28} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{32} : \frac{1}{7} $$

Now make it simple:

$$ \frac{1}{32} : \frac{1}{7} = 7 : 32 $$

Since number of molecules is in the same ratio as moles, the ratio of number of molecules is

$$ \boxed{7:32} $$

So, the correct option is C.

subject: Chemistry
q: A sample of Calcium phosphate Ca$_3$(PO$_4$)$_2$ contains 8 mol of O atoms. The number of mol of Ca atoms in the sample is :
question: A sample of Calcium phosphate Ca$_3$(PO$_4$)$_2$ contains 8 mol of O atoms. The number of mol of Ca atoms in the sample is :
solution:
In $ \text{Ca}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 $, count the atoms carefully.

One formula unit has:

  • Ca atoms $= 3$

  • O atoms $= 2 \times 4 = 8$

So, the ratio of Ca atoms to O atoms is

$$ \frac{\text{Ca}}{\text{O}} = \frac{3}{8} $$

Given that the sample contains $8$ mol of O atoms, the mol of Ca atoms will be

$$ \text{mol of Ca} = \frac{3}{8} \times 8 = 3 $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{3} $$

Option C

subject: Chemistry
q: 64 g of an organic compound has 24 g carbon and 8 g hydrogen and the rest is oxygen. The empirical formula of the compound is :
question: 64 g of an organic compound has 24 g carbon and 8 g hydrogen and the rest is oxygen. The empirical formula of the compound is :
solution:
Out of $64\ \text{g}$ compound,

  • Carbon $= 24\ \text{g}$

  • Hydrogen $= 8\ \text{g}$

  • Oxygen $= 64 - 24 - 8 = 32\ \text{g}$

Now convert these masses into moles:

$$ \text{Moles of C} = \frac{24}{12} = 2 $$

$$ \text{Moles of H} = \frac{8}{1} = 8 $$

$$ \text{Moles of O} = \frac{32}{16} = 2 $$

So the mole ratio is

$$ C : H : O = 2 : 8 : 2 $$

Divide by the smallest number, which is $2$:

$$ C : H : O = 1 : 4 : 1 $$

Hence the empirical formula is

$$ CH_4O $$

So, the correct option is A, $CH_4O$.

subject: Chemistry
q: If $1 \mathrm{dm}^3$ of a gas weights 2.5 g at STP, its gram-molecular weight (in gram) is :
question: If $1 \mathrm{dm}^3$ of a gas weights 2.5 g at STP, its gram-molecular weight (in gram) is :
solution:
At STP, $1$ mole of any gas occupies $22.4 \ \mathrm{dm}^3$.

Given that $1 \ \mathrm{dm}^3$ of the gas weighs $2.5 \ \mathrm{g}$.

So, $22.4 \ \mathrm{dm}^3$ of the gas will weigh

$$ 2.5 \times 22.4 = 56 \ \mathrm{g} $$

This is the gram-molecular weight of the gas.

$$ \boxed{56 \ \mathrm{g}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A sample of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ gas has the same mass as $2 \times 10^7$ molecules of $\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_8$. How many $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules are present in the sample?

(Report your answer as $X$, where Number of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules $=X \times 10^7$ )
question:
A sample of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ gas has the same mass as $2 \times 10^7$ molecules of $\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_8$. How many $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules are present in the sample?

(Report your answer as $X$, where Number of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules $=X \times 10^7$ )
solution:
Mass of one molecule is proportional to its molecular mass.

For $\mathrm{CH}_4$,

$$M(\mathrm{CH}_4)=12+4(1)=16$$

For $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_8$,

$$M(\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_8)=4(12)+8(1)=56$$

Given that the sample of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ has the same mass as $2\times 10^7$ molecules of $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_8$.

If number of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules is $n$, then

$$n \times 16 = 2\times 10^7 \times 56$$

So,

$$n=\frac{2\times 10^7 \times 56}{16}$$

$$n=2\times 10^7 \times \frac{7}{2}$$

$$n=7\times 10^7$$

So the number of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ molecules is $7\times 10^7$.

Hence,

$$X=7$$

subject: Chemistry
q: A sample of ammonium phosphate $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$ contains 3 mol of H atoms. The number of mol of O atoms in the sample is :
question: A sample of ammonium phosphate $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$ contains 3 mol of H atoms. The number of mol of O atoms in the sample is :
solution:
In $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3\mathrm{PO}_4$, the number of hydrogen atoms in one formula unit is:

$$ 3 \times 4 = 12 $$

So, $1$ mol of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3\mathrm{PO}_4$ contains $12$ mol of H atoms.

Given that the sample contains $3$ mol of H atoms, let the amount of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3\mathrm{PO}_4$ be $x$ mol.

Then,

$$ 12x = 3 $$

$$ x = \frac{3}{12} = \frac{1}{4}\text{ mol} $$

Now in one formula unit of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3\mathrm{PO}_4$, the number of oxygen atoms is $4$.

So, $\frac{1}{4}$ mol of $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3\mathrm{PO}_4$ will contain:

$$ 4 \times \frac{1}{4} = 1 \text{ mol of O atoms} $$

The number of mol of O atoms in the sample is:

$$ 1 \text{ mol} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: In an organic compound of molar mass $108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}$ and N atoms are present in $9: 1: 3.5$ by weight. Molecular formula can be, in the form of $\mathrm{C}_x \mathrm{H}_y \mathrm{~N}_z$, then sum of $x+y+z$ is :
question: In an organic compound of molar mass $108 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}$ and N atoms are present in $9: 1: 3.5$ by weight. Molecular formula can be, in the form of $\mathrm{C}_x \mathrm{H}_y \mathrm{~N}_z$, then sum of $x+y+z$ is :
solution:
Take the given mass ratio of elements:

$$\mathrm{C} : \mathrm{H} : \mathrm{N} = 9 : 1 : 3.5$$

Convert these masses into moles by dividing by atomic masses:

$$\frac{9}{12} : \frac{1}{1} : \frac{3.5}{14}$$

$$= 0.75 : 1 : 0.25$$

Now divide all by the smallest value $0.25$:

$$\frac{0.75}{0.25} : \frac{1}{0.25} : \frac{0.25}{0.25} = 3 : 4 : 1$$

So the empirical formula is:

$$\mathrm{C}_3\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{N}$$

Empirical formula mass:

$$3(12) + 4(1) + 1(14) = 36 + 4 + 14 = 54$$

Given molar mass is $108$:

$$\frac{108}{54} = 2$$

So molecular formula is:

$$\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_8\mathrm{N}_2$$

Now,

$$x+y+z = 6+8+2 = 16$$

So the answer is:

$$\boxed{16}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: 2 g of a hydrocarbon, one molecule of which contains 4 H -atoms, contains 0.05 gram-molecule of hydrocarbon. Find the number of C-atoms in one molecule of hydrocarbon.
question: 2 g of a hydrocarbon, one molecule of which contains 4 H -atoms, contains 0.05 gram-molecule of hydrocarbon. Find the number of C-atoms in one molecule of hydrocarbon.
solution:
Given:

  • Mass of hydrocarbon $= 2 \, \text{g}$

  • Amount of hydrocarbon $= 0.05$ gram-molecule, that is $0.05$ mole

  • One molecule contains $4$ hydrogen atoms

First find the molar mass of the hydrocarbon.

$$ \text{Molar mass} = \frac{\text{given mass}}{\text{number of moles}} = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40 \, \text{g mol}^{-1} $$

So, the molecular mass of the hydrocarbon is $40$.

Now suppose the hydrocarbon is $C_xH_4$.

Its molecular mass will be:

$$ 12x + 4(1) = 40 $$

$$ 12x + 4 = 40 $$

$$ 12x = 36 $$

$$ x = 3 $$

So the hydrocarbon is $C_3H_4$.

Hence, the number of carbon atoms in one molecule is

$$ \boxed{3} $$
